observations.recommendation:
1.13. We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to:
a. require the Inventory Committee to conduct exigent effort in
physical count on the property, plant and equipment to determine
the existence and condition of the all PPEs and its completeness in
order to reconcile RPCPPE vis a vis the accounting and property
records;
b. require the Municipal Accountant and the GSO to (i) maintain
subsidiary records for PPE items such as PPE ledger cards and
property cards, respectively, starting with current year acquisitions
until all items are covered; and (ii) reconcile the respective records
of the Accounting Office and Property Office on PPE; and
c. require the Municipal Accountant to prepare a depreciation
schedule for its depreciable assets.

2.5. We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to:
a. strictly observe the prescribed accounting guidelines and control
procedures on the accounting of semi-expendable expenses;
b. reclassify the said items to semi-expendable item as required by the
COA Circular by preparing appropriate adjusting entries and
require the property office to maintain Semi-expendable Ledger
Cardsin order to install adequate controls to the pave way for
establishing the actual amount of the semi-expendable expenses at
the end of the accounting period; and
c. prepared adjusting entries to move the said item to appropriate
expense account.

4.7. We recommended and the:
a. Municipal Treasurer agreed to post notice of delinquency for
property taxes at the main entrance of the municipal hall building
and in a publicly accessible and conspicuous place in each barangay
where these properties are located; seek assistance of the concerned
barangays for the issuance of Notice of Delinquency and conduct an
information dissemination regarding the importance of paying taxes
promptly and the consequences of non-payment of taxes; and if
warranted, enforce the provision of Section 254 (a) and (b) of the RA
7160 in the collection of delinquent taxes; and
b. Municipal Accountant agreed to reconcile with the office of the
Municipal Treasurer the variance noted.

5.12. We recommended and Local Chief Executive agreed to require the Treasurer
to:
a. instruct the taxpayers to fully and properly accomplish their duly
notarized applications for business permits and require the
submission of audited financial statements/income tax returns in
support of the gross receipts that they declare. Assessments should
be accurately computed based on the declared gross receipts. If in
the perception of the municipal officials concerned, there was under
declaration of gross receipts by taxpayers, the LGU may apply its
discretion to inspect the books of the taxpayer pursuant to Section
171 of the Local Government Code; and
b. maintain associated subsidiary ledgers and business index cards in
order to install adequate controls to pave the way for establishing the
actual amount of the business tax to be collected for each registered
business.

7.16. We recommended, and the Local Chief Executive agreed to instruct the:
(a) LDRRM Officer to: (i) include, under a separate caption, the list of projects
and activities charged to the unexpended LDRRMF from previous years in the
LDRRMIFP; and (ii) continue utilizing the fund for the implementation of
projects and activities related to disaster prevention and mitigation,
preparedness, response, rehabilitation, and recovery.
(b) Municipal Accountant to maintain subsidiary ledgers (SLs) for each type
of disaster response and rescue equipment.

8.16. We reiterated our previous year’s recommendation to the Local Chief
Executive (LCE):
a. through the Liga ng mga Barangay, encourage active participation
of barangay officials to support the solid waste management
program of the government by educating their respective
constituents on the proper segregation of reusable or recyclable
materials from compostable wastes;
b. through the ESMO, comply with the requirements of the law on
waste segregation by placing trash bins with proper labels and by
collecting only that garbage that is properly segregated;
c. recommend to the Sangguniang Bayan (SB) policies and possible
measures that will ensure full compliance by the barangays with the
provisions of R.A. No. 9003; and
d. initiate the implementation of Anti-Littering policy ordinances as
provided by Section 48 thereof.

10.6. We recommended and the LCE agreed to direct the Municipal Health Officer
to:
a. provide an explanation of the foregoing circumstances, with
justification, including, but not limited to the conduct of a needs
analysis;
b. conduct a thorough inspection and testing of the idle asset and
indicate in an inspection report whether such assets are functional,
for repair, or for disposal;
c. if possible, make necessary improvement on the said multi-purpose
vehicle to be able to make it serviceable and used in their medical
mission; and
d. henceforth, conduct a needs analysis prior to asset acquisitions so
that only necessary equipment will be procured, to ensure that
limited government funds are economically and effectively utilized.

13.10. We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to:
a. require the GAD Focal Person to comply with the provisions of PCW,
DILG, DBM and NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-01
and COA Circular No. 2014-001 particularly on the conduct of
gender audit and gender mainstreaming in the Municipality;
b. direct the Municipal Planning and Development Officer, together
with the GAD Focal Person, to exert effort in establishing a GAD
database which will serve as basis for gender-responsive planning,
programming and policy formulation;
c. furnish the audit team a copy of Certificate of Review from the
DILG;
d. adopt the DILG’s GAD-Plan and Budget Monitoring System
(PBMS);
e. furnish the team copy of the Executive Order on the composition of
the GFPS Technical Working group; and
f. require the institutionalization of the Monitoring and Evaluation
(MandE) Team to conduct annual monitoring of the
accomplishments and evaluation of the utilization and outcome of the
GAD budget in terms of its success in influencing the gender
responsive implementation of other PPAs of the LGU.

15.4. We recommended, and the OIC Municipal Treasurer agreed, to continue the
remittance of contributions and repayments of loan amortizations deducted from the
salaries of regular/permanent employees and job order personnel within the prescribed
period, in accordance with the provisions of RA No. 9679.

16.5. We recommended and the Accounting and Treasury Office agreed to continue
to comply with the deduction and remittance of PhilHealth contributions of its
employees within the prescribed period pursuant to Section 18 f Republic Act
No.7875 and its Implementing Rules and Regulations.

17.5. We recommend and the Management agreed to:: (a) ensure the continued
withholding of taxes and verify that all taxes withheld are fully and promptly remitted
to the Bureau of Internal Revenue (BIR) within the prescribed period, in strict
compliance with Revenue Memorandum Circular No. 23-2007; and (b) undertake
due diligence to ascertain the cause of the negative balance amounting to ₱192,368.91
in the General Fund and implement the necessary adjusting entries to rectify the
discrepancy. In the event that the negative balance is attributable to an over-
remittance, Management should coordinate with the BIR to initiate the appropriate
procedures for the refund or return of the over-remitted amount to the agency.

all concerned Municipal officials to (i) exert effort in the reproduction of titles on all lots owned by the Municipality and (ii) recognize same  in the books  using appropriate values; and
Municipal Accountant to submit the Schedule of Depreciation for Depreciable Assets and record depreciation on a monthly basis to establish  the accuracy of accounting estimates.

2.6.	We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to instruct the:
Municipal Treasurer to (i) furnish the monthly Schedule of Unreleased Checks to the Municipal Accountant; (ii) maintain a record of the date of release of all checks issued to the payee;
Municipal Accountant to draw journal entry to revert back the cash balance of unreleased checks at-year end, if any; and
Proper coordination between Treasury and Accounting Offices to monitor the actual status of checks issued by the Treasury so that appropriate adjustments could be made in a timely manner.

3.13.	We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to a) continue to utilize solely the 20% Development Fund for the purpose as enumerated under Item 3.2.5 of DBM-DOF-DILG Joint Memorandum Circular (JMC) No. 1; and b) direct the Local Development Council to (i) specify the projects, programs and activities under the Development Fund; and (ii) stop the practice of lump sum budgeting and inclusion of generic items in the Annual Investment Plan and Annual Budget.

4.17.	However,  we also recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to (a)  (i) implement without further delay those remaining projects not completed/implemented in CY 2022; and (ii) continue to monitor and supervise the effective and efficient implementation of projects funded from various source agencies within the timelines of the MOA, as far as feasible; and (b) require the Municipal Planning and Development Officer to update the uploaded data on the website and make the necessary posting as part of the public disclosure requirements.

5.18.	However, we recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to require the (a) MDRRMC to (i) prepare and submit the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund Investment Plan (LDRRMFIP) indicating the detailed PPAs and the corresponding appropriation classified as either Maintenance and Other Operating Expenses (MOOE) or Capital Outlay (CO) and under a separate caption, list of projects and activities charged to the unexpended LDRRMF of previous years for ease of monitoring of project implementation; and (ii) refrain from utilizing the fund other than the projects and activities for disaster prevention and mitigation, preparedness, response and rehabilitation and recovery; and (b) Municipal Accountant to maintain corresponding Subsidiary Ledger for each type of Disaster Response and Rescue Equipment.

7.10.	We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to  (a) require the GAD Focal Person to comply with the provisions of PCW, DILG, DBM and NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-01 and COA Circular No. 2014-001 particularly on the conduct of gender audit and gender mainstreaming in the Municipality; (b) direct the Municipal Planning and Development Officer, together with the GAD Focal Person, to exert effort in establishing a GAD database which will serve as basis for gender-responsive planning, programming and policy formulation; and (c) submit the documentary requirements supporting the GAD Accomplishment Report.

8.4.	We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to instruct the concerned officials to submit/furnish the Audit Team copies of Purchase Orders and all the supporting documents forming part thereof within five (5) days from the execution of a contract by the government pursuant to COA Circular Nos. 96-010 and 2009-001.

9.9.	We recommended and the General Services Officer agreed, as far as practicable and feasible, procure directly from the PS-DBM all anticipated common-use supplies, and resort to local suppliers using the prescribed modes of procurement only upon unavailability of supplies needed as certified by the proper  PS-DBM official.

10.10. Assessments should be accurately computed based on the declared gross receipts. If in the perception of the municipal officials concerned, there was under declaration of gross receipts by taxpayers, the LGU may apply its discretion to inspect the books of the taxpayer pursuant to Section 171 of the Local Government Code.

11.4.	We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the
Municipal Treasurer to stamp “PAID” all Disbursement Vouchers and their supporting documents to preclude the possible re-use of the same.

12.5.	We recommended and the Management agreed to instruct the (a) Municipal Accountant to continue deducting compulsory deduction from the fixed monthly compensation of the members and the personal share of the members and (b) the Municipal Treasurer to remit intact the amounts due to the GSIS within the prescribed period in adherence to Section 6 (b) of Republic Act No. 8291 and its Implementing Rules and Regulations; and (b) reconcile with the GSIS any difference noted.
12.6.	We also recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to exert efforts to trace the negative balance of ₱10,127.80 and effect adjustments to ensure the correctness of the balances reported in the financial statements.

13.5.	We commended the efforts of Management practice of remitting the amounts due the Home Development Mutual Fund or Pag-IBIG Fund within the prescribed period in accordance with Section 3(a) of Republic Act No. 9769 and its Implementing Rules and Regulations.

14.5.	We recommended and the Accounting and Treasury Office agreed to continue to comply with the deduction and remittance of PhilHealth contributions of its employees within the prescribed period pursuant to Section 18(b) of Republic Act No.7875 and its Implementing Rules and Regulations.

15.4.	We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to continue withholding taxes and check that all taxes withheld are remitted intact and immediately to BIR within the prescribed period as required by Revenue Memorandum Circular No. 23-2007 dated March 23, 2007.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive: 

require the GAD Focal Person to comply with the provisions of PCW, DILG, DBM and NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-01 and COA Circular No. 2014-001, particularly on the conduct of gender audit and gender mainstreaming in the Municipality; 

direct the Municipal Planning and Development Officer, together with the GAD Focal Person, to exert effort in establishing a GAD database which will serve as basis for gender-responsive planning, programming and policy formulation; and 

submit the documentary requirements supporting the GAD Accomplishment Report.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to exert effort in enforcing collection remedies allowed by the pertinent provisions of RA No. 7160, particularly the sale of delinquent properties through public auction. This will demonstrate the Municipality’s willpower and capability to enforce the laws on real property taxation and deter tax evasions that will result in increased collections and decreased tax delinquencies.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the concerned officials to submit/furnish the Audit Team copies of Contracts, Purchase Orders and all the supporting documents forming part thereof pursuant to COA Circular Nos. 96-010 and 2009-001.

We recommend and the Local Chief Executive agreed that the General Services Officer, as far as practicable and feasible, procure directly from the PS-DBM all anticipated common-use supplies, and resort to local suppliers using the prescribed modes of procurement only upon unavailability of supplies needed.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to instruct the Municipal Treasurer to immediately avail the use of the ATM payroll system in the payment of wages for the Municipal employees, including the Casual. This will protect not only the payroll funds from losses but also the lives of the Accountable Officer/s who do withdrawals of huge amount of cash from the bank.

Likewise, the use of such system will efficiently reduce the hours spent in manually paying the wages of personnel.

We recommended that Management: 

continue deducting and remitting intact the amounts due to the GSIS within the prescribed period in adherence to Section 6 (b) of Republic Act No. 8291 and its Implementing Rules and Regulations; and 

reconcile with the GSIS any difference noted.

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant exert efforts to trace the differences and effect adjustments to ensure the correctness of the balances reported in the financial statements.

We commended Management and also recommended that it sustain efforts in the unfailing remittance of the amounts due the Home Development Mutual Fund or Pag-IBIG Fund within the prescribed period in accordance with Section 3(a) of Republic Act No. 9769 and its Implementing Rules and Regulations. We also recommend that Management see to it that the amount remitted is equal to the contributions and loans deducted.

We recommended that the Accounting and Treasury Office continue to comply with the deduction and remittance of PhilHealth contributions of its employees within the prescribed period pursuant to Section 18(b) of Republic Act No.7875 and its Implementing Rules and Regulations. 

Likewise, we also recommended that the Municipal Accountant exert efforts to trace the differences and effect adjustments to ensure the correctness of the balances reported in the financial statements.

We recommended that Management: 

(a) continue to withhold taxes and check that all taxes withheld are remitted intact and immediately to the BIR within the prescribed period as required by Revenue Memorandum Circular No. 23-2007; and 

reconcile the difference between the records of the Municipality and the BIR.

We recommended and the Management agreed that the Municipality Engineer inform the audit team of the posting of project signboards and/or public notices within the time specified in the aforementioned circular for timely validation.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Budget Officer (a) explain its failure to comply with the rules set by LBC 131; and (b) require the concerned department to update the uploaded data on the website and make the necessary posting as part of their public disclosure requirements.
We also recommended that the LCE (a) require the Municipal Engineer, as the Head of the BAC, to provide justification on the delayed delivery of the of the above-mentioned project and implement an efficient monitoring system for all on-going projects; (b) advice the Municipal Accountant to impose liquidated damages on the above claims and to explain the advance payment of more than 15% to the winning bidder.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to (a) continue to invest in high-impact capital expenditure projects in the utilization of its 20% Development Fund; (b) ensure the effective and efficient implementation of the proposed/identified projects within their targeted completion period through close monitoring and supervision to maximize the developmental benefits and the early enjoyment thereof by the intended beneficiaries; and (c) to implement all identified projects/programs/activities of the 20% Development Fund in order to realize/attain the goals of the JMC No. 01 dated March 27, 2020 which is to contain the spread of COVID-19 and to provide basic services to the affected population.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive and heads of concerned implementing offices agreed to (i) implement without further delay those remaining projects not completed/implemented in CY 2022; and (ii) continue to monitor and supervise the effective and efficient implementation of projects funded from various source agencies within the timelines of the MOA, as far as feasible; and (b) Municipal Accountant and the Treasurer continue to be prompt in the return of excess funds to the source agencies upon completion or termination of the project.

We recommended that the Management (a) strictly observe the prescribed accounting guidelines and control procedures on the receipt, issuance, and storage/custodianship of supplies and materials; and (b) install adequate controls by maintaining a record of receipt and issuances to pave the way for establishing the cost of ending inventory at the end of the accounting period.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to require members of the Local School Board (LSB) (a) to submit quarterly and annual reports using the SEF Budget Accountability Form No. 1 on SEF utilization, to the DepEd CO, through appropriate channels, and copy furnish the local sanggunian concerned and Regional Offices of the DBM and DILG to promote and enhance transparency and accountability in the allocation and utilization of the SEF; and (b) to come up with a strategic prioritization plan in budgeting, allocating and utilizing of the SEF to public schools taking into consideration the most important needs of the students for the optimum attainment of the intent of the fund in accordance with DepEd-DDBM-DLG JC 2020-01 and 02.

Moreover, we reiterated our previous year’s recommendation and the Local Chief Executive agreed to (a) continue to  coordinate efforts with the barangay officials on the implementation of the five basic environmental laws on preserving and protecting the environment required under R.A. No. 9003; (b) enforce strictly the Sangguniang Bayan ordinance on the “no segregation, no collection” policy; and (c) intensify information drive and awareness campaign on the regulations on solid waste management in coordination with the officials in the Municipality and barangay officials.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to prepare the necessary adjusting entries to record the appropriate accounts. It is further recommended that future transaction be properly recorded according to the prescribed accounts in COA Circular No. 2015-009 in order to avoid misstatements and fairly present the balances in the financial statement.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive and all officials concerned (a) institute necessary control in the issuance of gas/diesel fuel to all of its government-owned motor service vehicles to regulate fuel consumption caused by unofficial trips; and (b) accomplish trip tickets by supplying all data called for therein and submit the Monthly Report of Fuel Consumption and Monthly Report of Officials Travels for proper monitoring and control.

We recommended that the (a) Vice-Mayor and Sangguniang Bayan Members observe the prohibition against expenses for reception and entertainment; (b) justify the incurrence of meals and snacks which are considered impractical and unnecessary; and (c) stop the practice of reimbursing/charging of meals and snacks that are not in accordance with the existing rules and regulations.
Moreover, we also recommended that the Accountant and the Budget Officer ensure that the meals and snacks served during ordinary meeting, monthly conference, regular sessions and for the entertainment of guests and visitors are in accordance with the existing rules and regulations.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to require the (a) MDRRMC to prepare a comprehensive Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund Investment Plan (LDRRMFIP) indicating the (i) detailed PPAs and the corresponding appropriation classified as either Maintenance and Other Operating Expenses (MOOE) or Capital Outlay (CO); (ii) under a separate caption, the list projects and activities charged to the unexpended LDRRMF of previous years for ease of monitoring of project implementation; (b) MDRRMC ensure that the LDRRMF be strictly utilized for the implementation of projects and activities for disaster prevention and mitigation, preparedness, response and rehabilitation and recovery; (c) Municipal Accountant to (i) comply with the monthly submission of the Report on Sources and Utilization of DRRMF to the other reporting agencies required by the COA Circular 2012-002; (ii) and maintain corresponding Subsidiary Ledger for each type of Disaster Response and Rescue Equipment; and (iii) ensure that the amount and details of the unexpended balance of the LDRRMF is discussed in the Notes to Financial Statements.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the GAD Focal Person to comply with the provisions of PCW, DILG, DBM and NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-01 and COA Circular No. 2014-001 particularly on the conduct of gender audit and gender mainstreaming in the Municipality.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (a) assess its existing organizational structure so that the ideal number of plantilla personnel will be established, thereby, avoiding the hiring of excessive number of job order personnel; (b) resort the hiring of job order personnel based on necessity in order to avoid waste of much needed funds, as prescribed in Section 76 of RA 7160; and (c) strictly adhere to the policies and guidelines in the hiring of personnel under job order as provided in CSC Memorandum No. 17, s. 2002 promulgating CSC Resolution No. 02-0790 dated June 5, 2002.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to (a) improve compliance with the regulation on the rendition of accounts and financial reports in order not to delay the verification of accounts and post-audit activities; (b) observe the due dates of submission of the required financial reports and documents to the Audit Team and (c) submit to the office the remaining financial reports for the year 2021.

However, we also recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to continue to enforce collection remedies as allowed by the pertinent provisions of RA No. 7160, particularly the sale of delinquent properties through public auction. This will demonstrate the Municipality’s willpower and capability to enforce the laws on real property taxation and deter tax evasions.

We reiterated our recommendations that the General Services Officer, as far as practicable and feasible, procure directly from the PS-DBM all anticipated common-use supplies and resort to procurement from local suppliers using the prescribed modes only upon unavailability of supplies needed.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to instruct the concerned officials to submit/furnish the Audit Team copies of notices of deliveries and Contracts, Purchase Orders and all supporting documents forming part thereof pursuant to COA Circular Nos. 96-010 and 2009-001, respectively.

We recognized the sincere efforts of the officials in addressing the waste management issues of the Municipality. 

Moreover, we reiterated our previous year’s recommendation and the Management agreed that the Local Chief Executive

(a) continue to  coordinate efforts with the barangay officials on the implementation of the five basic environmental laws on preserving and protecting the environment required under R.A. No. 9003;

observations.reason: With that in coordination with the barangay officials with regards to the Ecological Solid Waste Management Act of 2000 and the approved Ten Year Solid Waste Management Plan of Sasmuan with a NSWMC Resolution No.1332 series of 2019, the LGU collection service will expand to the whole municipality. The public market and commercial establishments along the national highway and even among institutions will be continuously serviced by the LGUs collection crew thru the office the MENRO. We always instructed the Barangay Officials especially the Barangay Chairman to be strictly implement and adopt the Ecological Solid Waste Management Act of 2000 and the “no segregation , no collection policy” thru the Ordinance No. 05-S-2015 an ordinance enacting the Comprehensive Solid waste Management Ordinance of the Municipality of Sasmuan. The ordinance aims to adopt a systematic, comprehensive and ecological solid waste management program that shall ensure the protection of public health and environment. The Ordinance ensures proper segregation, collection, storage, treatment and disposal of solid waste through the formulation and adaption of best eco-waste products.
